coredata.dc:title: Effect of surface modification on optical and electronic properties of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rani, Priya
Dalal, Ranjeet
Srivastava, Sunita
coredata.dc:description: Optical and electronic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be modified either by introducing heteroatoms on their surface or by covalently linking these with certain groups. In this work, time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) with functional B3LYP/6-31G* has been used to study the effects of boron doping and surface-modified configurations of GQDs on the HOMO-LUMO gap and absorption spectra. The photoluminescence properties and quantum yield of GQDs have also been explored. Doping as well as a surface modification with boron configurations resulted in a decrease in the band gap and a red shift in absorption and photoluminescence/fluorescence spectra. As compared to surface modified GQDs, the presence of B atom in the edge modified GQDs has a pronounced effect on the tuning of the energy bandgap. Also, BCO2 is found to affect the electrical structures of GQDs more significantly as compared to BC2O. The fluorescence of surface-modified GQD is found fall in the near-infrared region with a quantum yield of 29 % rendering them suitable for bio-imaging applications. The results thus obtained are in good consonance with those reported in the literature.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the HPCC, Panjab University, Chandigarh, for facilitating the Gaussian 09 Package for carrying out the calculations. The authors are also thankful to the Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar, for providing the necessary computational facilities. Priya Rani also acknowledges GJUS&T for providing financial support in the form of University Research Scholarship ID SC/ST/21/349- 357, Priya Rani.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the HPCC, Panjab University, Chandigarh, for facilitating the Gaussian 09 Package for carrying out the calculations. The authors are also thankful to the Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar, for providing the necessary computational facilities. Priya Rani also acknowledges GJUS&T for providing financial support in the form of University Research Scholarship ID SC/ST/21/349- 357, Priya Rani.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of edge and surface modified GQDs. The yellow circle indicates the doped boron atom in the core region. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The absorption spectrum of (a) edge and (b) surface modified GQDs.
Representation of H-1, H, L and L + 1 energy levels of all GQDs.

The HOMO and LUMO surface plots of edge and surface modified GQDs. The red and green colors represent the positive and negative lobes respectively. The yellow circle indicates the position of the boron atom. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of (a) GQD-BC2O2-e and (b) GQD-B2-BCO22-s.
Cohesive energy of all the considered GQDs.

head.ce:title: Effect of surface modification on optical and electronic properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar 125001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar 125001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, Central University of Haryana, Jant-Pali, Mahenderagarh 123029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, Central University of Haryana, Jant-Pali, Mahenderagarh 123029, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics and Astrophysics, Central University of Haryana, Jant-Pali, Mahenderagarh 123029, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optical and electronic properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be modified either by introducing heteroatoms on their surface or by covalently linking these with certain groups. In this work, time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) with functional B3LYP/6-31G* has been used to study the effects of boron doping and surface-modified configurations of GQDs on the HOMO-LUMO gap and absorption spectra. The photoluminescence properties and quantum yield of GQDs have also been explored. Doping as well as a surface modification with boron configurations resulted in a decrease in the band gap and a red shift in absorption and photoluminescence/fluorescence spectra. As compared to surface modified GQDs, the presence of B atom in the edge modified GQDs has a pronounced effect on the tuning of the energy bandgap. Also, BCO2 is found to affect the electrical structures of GQDs more significantly as compared to BC2O. The fluorescence of surface-modified GQD is found fall in the near-infrared region with a quantum yield of 29 % rendering them suitable for bio-imaging applications. The results thus obtained are in good consonance with those reported in the literature.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: We have attached Electronic Supporting Information where we have listed five Tables depicting data on Absorption energies, wavelengths, and oscillator strengths of GQDs and corresponding figures drawn.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its distinct two-dimensional (2D) crystal structure, graphene has demonstrated intriguing physicochemical properties, and its possible usage in technological systems [1] and molecular sensing [2] resulting in an immense interest in its study. Graphene is a monolayer of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and has an infinite Bohr radius with zero band gap [3,4]. Consequently no photoluminescence could be observed from graphene and thus restricting its application to optoelectronics. To tailor the properties of graphene effectively, the bandgap of the graphene sheet could be constructively engineered. Due to graphene's infinite Bohr radius, quantum confinement phenomena could be observed only for finite-sized graphene resulting in graphene nanostructures / GQDs characterized by a finite band gap. GQDs exhibit exceptional optical and electrical properties as a consequence of the significant quantum confinement [5,6]. GQDs have noteworthy advantages of cost-effectiveness, significant water solubility, persistent fluorescence, adjustable bandgap, nontoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [7–9]. Therefore, GQDs are perfectly suitable candidates for replacing traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [10,11] which are very costly, toxic, and exhibit low biocompatibility.

By employing appropriate fabrication and post-processing techniques, the properties of graphene nanostructures/GQDs could be modified in terms of their geometry and elemental composition in addition to their size, surface, and nature as demonstrated by both theoretical and experimental research [12]. The Ability to modulate the band gap and fluorescence (FL) [13] of GQDs extending from ultraviolet to near-infrared, makes them useful for a variety of applications, including bio-imaging, photovoltaics [14], and drug delivery [15]. Surface engineering with heteroatom doping is one of the several successful approaches for modifying its optical properties. For example, with hexylamines these GQDs are observed to have enhanced luminescent property [16]. By grafting hexylamines to the surface of GQDs,their surface properties could get modified changing their hydrophilic behavior to hydrophobic. These surface-modified GQDs could successfully stabilize submicron-sized colloidal particles as well as oil-in-water Pickering emulsions in mini-emulsion polymerization. Due to the GQDs luminescence, these GQD surfactants could generate luminous particles, which have significant potential for its usage in fluorescent sensors and imaging among other applications. Surface functionalization of GQDs with polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS), poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG), and hexadecyl amine (HDA) has also been proven to be a very good strategy to improve the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of GQDs to a great extent [17]. The GQDs were also modified to improve their biosensing and antibiofilm capabilities [18]. Numerous heteroatoms including sulfur [19,20], boron [21], nitrogen [22], phosphorus [23], and oxygen [24] have been employed as dopants in GQDs to improve their optoelectronic properties. In comparison to other heteroatoms, a boron atom with fewer valence electrons than a carbon atom has a comparable size and hence could be easily embedded in the framework of carbon atoms resulting in a minor structural disorder. Doping GQDs with B (B-GQDs) has therefore gained a lot of significant interest due to its strange behaviours and surprising characteristics like high crystallinity, uniform size, high quantum yield (QY), and excitation-independent fluorescence [25]. It has been revealed that boron-doped graphene quantum dots could provide an effective photoanode for dye-sensitive photovoltaic cells [26] with a power conversion efficiency of 3.7 %. B-GQDs could also act as fluorescence detectors for the manufacture of sensing devices for the phosphate or Fe3+ ion as well as biosensors [25]. DFT and TD-DFT have also been used during last few years to determine the properties of GQDs for the last few years. B-GQDs have a lower band gap concerning B-free GQDs as demonstrated by TD-DFT [27,28] and therefore, results in a red shift in absorption spectra. The surface of GQDs has also been modified by selective boronization. The surface modified with BCO2 showed a blue shift in absorption spectra while other boron-configuration exhibited redshift [29]. This is a very distinct behaviour revealed by BCO2 configured GQDs. However, their effect has been examined only on absorption properties and not on emission spectra which is very essential for understanding the optical behaviour of this material.

In this work, we have investigated how surface and edge modifications with and without doping could affect the optical characteristics of GQDs. The modifications in GQDs have been divided into two categories, viz, edge and surface-modified GQDs with borinic acid (-BC2O) and boronic acid (-BCO2) groups respectively. The structures are further doped with boron atoms represented as e-B-GQDs and s-B-GQDs to study the impact of doping on these different properties. Though their impact on absorption characteristics has been established earlier, there is presently no research data available that could explain as to how these modifications affect emission properties which are very significant in ascertaining the practical use of GQDs in various applications. Therefore, our primary interest is in analyzing FL and related quantum yield (QY) of surface and edge-modified GQDs with B configurations. The obtained absorption spectra and band gap values are in good agreement with the previously reported work [29]. Further, the emission properties and QY have also been analyzed. The results thus obtained could be found helpful in bio-imaging and other optoelectronic applications.

The geometrical and electrical parameters of surface and edge-modified GQDs have been evaluated using the Gaussian 09 software. The calculations have been performed for the GQDs dissolved in the water by using Becke three parameters hybrid functional with Lee-Yang-Perdew correlation functional (B3LYP) with a 6-31G* basis set [30]. For calculating absorption properties, the ground state has been optimized by density function theory (DFT), and the fully relaxed structure has been obtained with no imaginary value of frequency and hence confirming the stability of the structure. The first 25 states have been used for the calculations based on the optimized ground state. For fluorescence emission, the lowest excited state has been optimized by using time-dependent DFT in the optimized ground state geometry. The solvent effects have been included using a polarization continuum model (PCM). The software GaussView (version 5) [31] has been used to visualize molecular orbitals using the outputs of the TD-DFT computations from previous calculations. Multiwfn software has been used to plot the graph of the absorption and fluorescence spectra [32].

The optimized structures of edge-modified GQDs with borinic acid (GQD-BC2O2-e (e-GQD) and GQD-B2-BC2O2-e (e-B-GQD)) and surface-modified GQDs with boronic acid (GQD-BCO22-S (s-GQD) and GQD-B2-BCO22-S (s-B-GQD)) are shown in Fig. 1 . The CC bond length is found to be in the range 1.41–1.45 Å which is in consonance with the experimentally measured value (1.44) of bond length [3]. The measured value of C-B bond length is 1.49–1.52 Å in the core region and 1.67 Å for the boron atom attached to the surface of GQDs. The bond length increases when the C atom is replaced by a B atom because of the larger size of the B than that of the C atom [33,34].

The stability of the structure is determined by measuring the cohesive energy per atom. The equation for the calculation of cohesive energy, Ec, is.
E c
=
E
doped / u n d o p e d G Q D
-
N c
E c
-
N X
E X
-
N H
E H
N C
+
N X
+
N H

Where X represents the B, O atoms, Ec represents the cohesive energy of the structure,
E
doped / u n d o p e d G Q D
denote the total energy of various doped and undoped GQDs, EC, EX and EH are the total energy of C, X, and H atoms (atomic energy), respectively. The total number of atoms of C, X, and H are represented by NC, NX, and NH respectively. The stability of structures is indicated by the negative value of cohesive energy. The value of Ec for all GQDs is calculated by using this equation and is listed in Table 1 . The negative value of Ec of all the structures verifies that the structure used for the calculations is stable. From the Table 1, it is clear that the cohesive energy of s-GQD is comparatively lower than that of s-B-GQD indicating it’s more stability. The s-B-GQD contains two additional boron atoms than that of s-GQD which leads to a decrease in the symmetry of sp2 hybridized C-atoms in GQD by converting it into sp3 hybridization in the surrounding of the boron atom and therefore results in lesser stability than that of symmetric configuration. Similarly, e-GQD is more stable than e-B-GQD.

This section describes the effect of the surface modifications on absorption, FL properties, and their QY. Since the absorption spectrum can precisely represent changes in the energy states in the GQDs with heteroatom doping, understanding these variations in the energy states is particularly helpful in explaining the FL emission modes of the GQDs. The absorption spectrum of edge and surface-modified GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The absorption spectra of pure GQD shows an absorption peak at 358 nm which corresponds to the S0 → S5 transition and is very close to the experimentally measured value (360 nm) [35]. The absorption maxima of edge-modified GQD with the BC2O shows a red shift and considerably reduced absorption strength in comparison to the pure GQD with a maximum contribution of S0 → S6 transition. This redshift is produced by the electron deficiency of the boron atom. The reduced absorption strength is because of the reduction of delocalized π-π* transitions in the core region of GQDs as a consequence of the low composition of the carbon sp2 region in e-B-GQD and s-B-GQD. More importantly, the decrease in absorption intensity is more in e-B-GQD than e-GQD because of the larger reduction C-sp2 region in this case. The shoulder edge around 450 nm in the absorption spectrum of e-B-GQD which is due to boron doping in the graphene hexagonal lattice and has also been confirmed experimentally [36,37]. In contrast, surface modification with BCO2 exhibits a completely different behaviour. Their absorption spectrum shows a blue shift in comparison to pure GQD and the maximum absorption peak appeared at 329 nm for s-GQD and 335 nm for s-B-GQD, with the highest contribution of S0 → S22 and S0 → S20 transitions respectively. The absorption spectra of s-GQDs exhibit a prominent absorption of around 320 nm because of a higher proportion of boronic acid [29,38]. The B-atom conjugated to C-atom in the boronic acid results in the change of the orbital hybridization of carbon atoms from sp2 to sp3 and accounts for this blue shift of the absorption spectra of s-GQDs. Similarly, a blue shift is observed for s-B- as evident from Fig. 2(b). The absorption edge at ∼442 nm in s-B-GQD is because of the presence of boron in the core region of QDs. The decrease in absorption strength after surface modification is due to the same reason as explained above. The absorption energy, wavelength, and oscillator strength of the first 25 energy states of all GQDs are represented in Tables S1–S5 in Electronic Supporting Information. The change in the energy and wavelength of all the 25 energy states have also been represented graphically in Figs. S1 and S2. It is clear from the tables that edge and surface modifications result in the expected decrease of energy of all the states.

The energy band gap is calculated as the difference between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO (L)) and the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO (H)). To show the impacts of various doping patterns with varied B configurations in GQDs, the H-1, H, L, and L + 1 energies of GQDs are illustrated in Fig. 3 . The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of surface and edge-modified GQDs show a noticeable change in contrast to pure GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3, the electron-withdrawing configurations containing the B atom (BCO2 and BC2O) lead to a decrease in the L energy levels of edge and surface-modified GQDs. The behaviours of the BCO2 group modification on the surface of GQD are considerably distinct from the other modifications in GQDs. The calculated energy band gap changes are opposite to that due to BC2O modification, which results in the change in the GQDs' absorption spectral properties. It is significant to examine that the surface modification with BCO2 has a larger impact on tuning the band gap of GQDs than that of the edge modification with BC2O. The basic and essential responsible parameter for controlling the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQDs is orbital hybridization. When the graphene sheet attaches to the BCO2 group, the carbon atoms’ sp2 hybridization transforms into an sp3 leading to a reduction in sp2 hybridization and consequentially decreasing the band gap. When the B atom is present in the core region, the band gap of GQD bonded with BC2O decreases more, whereas this effect is completely the opposite for GQD bonded with BCO2. This suggests that compared to surface-modified GQD, the presence of the B atom in edge-modified GQDs has a higher impact on tuning the energy band gap.

The electronic structure of GQDs could be tuned because of their geometrical distortion in modifying their surface. To completely analyze the effect of geometry, the H and L surfaces of all GQDs have been displayed in Fig. 4 . As depicted in Fig. 4(a), the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of pure GQD exhibit a clear π orbital property. It is clear from Fig. 4 (b–e) that compared to the other doped GQDs, the spatial distribution of wave functions for s-GQD and s-B-GQD appears to be completely different. The enhanced localization and orbital properties of the H and L in the s-GQD and s-B-GQD demonstrate that the sp2 conjugated C experienced a transition into the sp3 configuration as a result of the BCO2 modification on the surface of the GQDs. This result provides more evidence to support the fact discussed above that BCO2 affects the electrical structures of GQDs more significantly than BC2O.

The emission properties have also been analyzed by optimizing the structures in the first excited state by using TD-DFT. The fluorescence wavelength of GQD has been calculated to be λ = 444.01 nm corresponding to the S1 → S0 transition with an oscillator strength (O. S.) of 0.00 implying zero probability of fluorescence emission from pure GQD. This is due to its high molecular symmetry [39]. However, after modifying these structures with BCO2 and BC2O groups, a non-zero O. S. is obtained for the S1 → S0 transition implying the possibility of fluorescence emission and thus satisfying Kasha’s rule. The fluorescence wavelength for e-B-GQD and s-GQD is found to be ∼32037 nm and ∼2873 nm, respectively. However, these do not find practical applications as they do not fall in the visible or IR range of the electromagnetic spectrum. Therefore, the fluorescence (FL) spectra of the remaining two have been plotted and used for calculating the FL QY. The FL spectrum for these two QDs has been shown in Fig. 5 .

It is clear from the figure that the FL wavelength of e-GQD is ∼463 nm which lies in the visible region. And the FL wavelength of s-B-GQD is ∼1026 nm which is in the infrared region. The QY is calculated by approximating the number of the absorbed photons to the area under the absorption curve and the number of emitted photons to the area under the fluorescence curve [40]. There, the formula used for the calculation of the QY is.
QY =
NumberofemittedPhotons
NumberoftheabsorbedPhotons
≈
areaunderthefluorescencecurve
areaundertheabsorptioncurve

The bare GQD have S1 state as a dark state (i.e. this state has zero oscillator strength (O.S.)) implying that photon absorption does not take place in this state and in addition they also do not radiate from these states (zero O.S. in emission process also), which indicates that the bare GQDs have negligible QY. The calculated FL QY of e-GQD (∼463 nm) is 4.6 % whereas for s-B-GQD (∼1026 nm), it is 29.4 %, which is a good achievement, promising their usefulnessl for the optoelectronic and bio-imaging applications respectively.

In summary, the absorption and emission properties of the surface and edge-modified GQDs have been analyzed with boronic and borinic acid respectively. The effects of surface and edge modification have also been studied on B-GQDs. The absorption spectra of edge-modified GQDs exhibited a red shift due to the presence of an electron-deficient boron atom resulting in the introduction of the energy levels in between the π-π* levels of C-atom while the absorption spectra of surface-modified GQDs exhibited a blue shift concerning the pure GQD because of the dominance of –BCO2 group embodying larger contribution of the electron-rich oxygen atom. The oscillator strength of the S1 → S0 transition for the pure GQD is found to be 0.0 whereas, with these modifications, a non-zero O. S. for S1 → S0 transition is obtained for all the QDs resulting in the fluorescence emission. The calculated FL QY of s-B-GQD is 29 % with FL emission wavelength ∼1026 nm thus making them a viable alternative for applications involving bio-imaging.

Priya Rani: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Ranjeet Dalal: Writing - review & editing. Sunita Srivastava: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Software, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the HPCC, Panjab University, Chandigarh, for facilitating the Gaussian 09 Package for carrying out the calculations. The authors are also thankful to the Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar, for providing the necessary computational facilities. Priya Rani also acknowledges GJUS&T for providing financial support in the form of University Research Scholarship ID SC/ST/21/349- 357, Priya Rani.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.155379.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiempirical modeling of the effects of the intrinsic and extrinsic optical phonons on the performance of the graphene-based devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jalalvandi, S. Darbari, and M. K. M. Farshi, “Semiempirical modeling of the effects of the intrinsic and extrinsic optical phonons on the performance of the graphene-based devices,” Scientific Reports, 12, 10417 (2022).
M. Machado, A. M. L. Oliveira, G. A. Silva, D. B. Bitoque, J. T. Ferreira, L. A. Pinto, and Q. Ferreira, “Graphene Biosensors—A Molecular Approach,” Nanomaterials, 12, 1624 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anomalous behaviors of visible luminescence from graphene quantum dots: interplay between size and shape

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kim, S. W. Hwang, M. K. Kim, D. Y. Shin, et al, “Anomalous Behaviors of Visible Luminescence from Graphene Quantum Dots: Interplay between Size and Shape,” ACS Nano, 6, 8203 (2012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Symmetry classification of energy bands in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Kogan, and V. U. Nazarov, “Symmetry classification of energy bands in graphene,” Phys. Rev. B, 85, 115418 (2012).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: P. Rani, R. Dalal, S. Srivastava, Electronic and optical properties of single benzene ring graphene quantum dots, in: Proceedings of the 65th DAE Solid State Physics Symposium, Vol. 55, 2021, pp. 562.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chaotic Dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. A. Ponomarenko, F. Schedin, M. I. Katsnelson, R. Yang, E. W. Hill, K. S. Novoselovand, A. K. Geim, “Chaotic Dirac Billiard in Graphene Quantum Dots,” Science, 320, 356 (2008).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for bioimaging and drug delivery applications: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. C. Biswas, M. T. Islam, P. K. Nandy, and M. M. Hossain, “Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) for Bioimaging and Drug Delivery Applications: A Review,” ACS Materials Lett., 3, 889 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots and their applications in bioimaging, biosensing, and therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chung, R. A. Revia, and M. Zhang, “Graphene Quantum Dots and Their Applications in Bioimaging, Biosensing, and Therapy,” Adv. Mater., 33, 1904362 (2019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in graphene quantum dot-based optical and electrochemical (bio)analytical sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kalkal, S. Kadian, R. Pradhan, G. Manik, and G. Packirisamy, “Recent advances in graphene quantum dot-based optical and electrochemical (bio)analytical sensors,” Mater. Adv., 2, 5513 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of electronic and optical properties of quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Dalal, and S. Srivastava, “Study of electronic and optical properties of quantum dots,” Appl. Nanosci., 12, 2127 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ab initio study of optical properties of ZnS quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Singh, and S. Srivastava, “Ab initio study of optical properties of ZnS quantum dots,” Materials Today Proceedings, 46, 5817 (2021)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, applications, and prospects of graphene quantum dots: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ghaffarkhah, E. Hosseini, M. Kamkar, A. A. Sehat, S. Dordanihaghighi, A. Allahbakhsh, C. Kuur, and M. Arjmand, “Synthesis, Applications, and Prospects of Graphene Quantum Dots: A Comprehensive Review,” Small, 18, 202102683 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward highly efficient luminescence in graphene quantum dots for optoelectronic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yoon, M. Park, J. Kim, T. G. Novak, S. Lee, and S. Jeon, “Toward highly efficient luminescence in graphene quantum dots for optoelectronic applications”, Chem. Phys. Rev., 2, 031303 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their applications in drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhao, X. Song, Y. Liu, Y. Fu, L. Ye, N. Wang, F. Wang, L. Li, M. Mohammadniaei, M. Zhang, Q. Zhang, and J. Liu, “Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and their applications in drug delivery,” J. Nanobiotechnology, 18, 142 (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based quantum dots for photovoltaic devices: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kim, J. K. Dash, P. Kumar, and R. Patel, “Carbon-Based Quantum Dots for Photovoltaic Devices: A Review,” ACS Appl. Electron. Mater., 4, 27 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface engineering of graphene quantum dots and their applications as efficient surfactants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. H. Cho, H. Yang, D. J. Kang, and B. J. Kim, “Surface Engineering of Graphene Quantum Dots and Their Applications as Efficient Surfactants,” ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 7, 8615 (2015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quenching-resistant solid-state photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: reduction of π−π stacking by surface functionalization with POSS, PEG, and HDA

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Park, Y. Jeong, H. S. Kim, W. Lee, S. H. Nam, S. Lee, H. Yoon, J. Kim, S. Yoo, and S. Jeon, “Quenching-Resistant Solid-State Photoluminescence of Graphene Quantum Dots: Reduction of π−π Stacking by Surface Functionalization with POSS, PEG, and HDA”, Adv. Funct. Mater., 2102741 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Post-synthetic modification of graphene quantum dots bestows enhanced biosensing and antibiofilm ability: efficiency facet

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Agrawal, D. Bhagel, P. Mishra, D. Prasad, and E. Kohli, “Post-synthetic modification of graphene quantum dots bestows enhanced biosensing and antibiofilm ability: efficiency facet,” RSC Adv., 12, 12310 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Decoration of green synthesized S, N-GQDs and CoFe 2O4 on halloysite nanoclay as a natural substrate for electrochemical hydrogen storage application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. G. Arani, M. S. Niasari, “Decoration of green synthesized S, N-GQDs and CoFe 2O4 on halloysite nanoclay as a natural substrate for electrochemical hydrogen storage application,” Scientific Reports, 12, 8103 (2022).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kadian, G. Manik, A. Kalkal, M. Singh, and R. Chauhan, “Effect of sulfur doping on fluorescence and quantum yield of graphene quantum dots: an experimental and theoretical investigation,” Nanotechnology, 30, 435704 (2019).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective detection of dopamine with boron and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Chatterjee, P. Nath, S. Kadian, A. Kumar, V. Kumar, P. Roy, G. Manik, and S. Satapathi, “Highly sensitive and selective detection of dopamine with boron and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots,” Scientific Reports, 12, 9061 (2022).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Li, Y. Du, M. Maab, and J. Zhang, “Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots coated with molecularly imprinted polymers as a fluorescent sensor for selective determination of warfarin,” New J. Chem, 46, 7537 (2022).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Guo, H. Wu, M. Li, T. Tang, J. Wen, and X. Li, “Phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots loaded on TiO2 for enhanced photodegradation,” Applied Surface Science, 526, 146724 (2020).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and oxygen co-doped graphene quantum dots with high capacitance performance for micro-supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, L. Cao, P. Qin, X. Liu, Z. Chen, L. Wang, D. Pan, and M. Wu, “Nitrogen and oxygen co-doped graphene quantum dots with high capacitance performance for micro-supercapacitors,” Carbon, 139, 67 (2018).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ge, J. He, C. Ma, J. Liu, F. Xi, and X. Dong, “One-step synthesis of boron-doped graphene quantum dots for fluorescent sensors and biosensors,” Talanta, 119, 581 (2019).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. M. Prabhagar, P. Kumar, C. Takahashi, S. Kundu, T. N. Narayanan, and D. K. Pattanayak, “Boron-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots: An Efficient Photoanode for Dye-Sensitized Solar Cell,” New J. Chem., 43, 14313 (2019).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Feng, H. Dong, B. Pang, F. Shao, C. Zhang, L. Yu, and L. Dong, “Theoretical Study on Optical and Electronic Properties of Graphene Quantum Dots Doped with Heteroatoms,” Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys., 20, 15244 (2018).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Feng, Q. Guo, N. Song, H. Liu, H. Dong, Y. Chen, L. Yu, and L. Dong, “Density functional theory study on optical and electronic properties of co-doped graphene quantum dots based on different nitrogen doping patterns,” Diamond & Related Materials, 113, 108264 (2021).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Feng, H. Dong, B. Pang, Y. Chen, L. Yu, and L. Dong, “Tuning Electronic and Optical Properties of Graphene Quantum Dots by selective Boronization,” J. Mater. Chem. C, 7, 237 (2019).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Schumacher, “Photophysics of graphene quantum dots: Insights from electronic structure calculations,” Phys. Rev. B, 83, 081417 (2011).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Lu, F. Chen, “Multiwfn: A multifunctional wavefunction analyser,” J. Computational Chem., 33, 580 (2012).
